concise:
Ayah 1–5 · linked video: 01. Al-Qadr (Ayah 1-5)

Surah Al-Qadr: The Night of Power

Ayah 1

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ

Allah ﷻ begins this remarkable surah by declaring: “Indeed, We sent it down in the Night of Power.” The opening carries profound weight in how Allah ﷻ speaks of the revelation. Notice that He says “We sent it down” rather than “We sent Our angel down to you.” By removing Jibreel (AS) from this equation, Allah ﷻ takes direct credit for the revelation, making it even more personal and direct. The words are His own, sent down specifically in this blessed night.

The pronoun “it” (هُ) in أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ is especially significant. This is what Arabic linguists call a pronoun of familiarity. When you say “Did you watch it?” or “Did you see it?” to someone, they either have no idea what you’re talking about, or both of you are thinking of the same thing. When Allah ﷻ says “We sent it down,” He’s indicating that the Quran had already become such a phenomenon in Makkah that it was on everyone’s mind. When people heard “it,” they couldn’t think of anything other than the Quran. Even the enemies of Islam knew exactly what “it” referred to. The Quran had permeated and flooded the city of Makkah so completely that it didn’t even need to be spelled out by name. This is the power and grandeur of the Quran captured in a single pronoun.

The phrase إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ (“Indeed, We sent it down”) has been understood by scholars in different ways. Ibn Abbas (RA) held a view that many scholars believe he learned directly from the Prophet ﷺ himself: that Allah ﷻ sent the entire Quran from the seventh heaven to the first heaven in one night—this Night of Power. Then, from that place in the first heaven, it was revealed gradually to the Prophet ﷺ over twenty-three years. The complete revelation came down in one night, and then the detailed, gradual revelation unfolded over the Prophet’s mission.

Scholars are inclined to believe Ibn Abbas (RA) learned this from the Messenger ﷺ himself because this is a matter of the unseen. A companion cannot speak with confidence about what happens in the seventh heaven or the first heaven unless he heard it from the one who was informed about the unseen—the Prophet ﷺ. While Ibn Abbas (RA) doesn’t quote the Prophet ﷺ directly, scholars consider this knowledge to be traced back to him.

Another interpretation is that “We sent it down” means “We began sending it down”—that the process of revelation to the Prophet ﷺ started in this night. The pronoun could also refer back to the previous surah, Surah Al-Alaq, connecting that first revelation with this Night of Power.

Some have suggested that Laylat al-Qadr refers to the middle of Sha’ban, citing narrations from Aisha (RA) about the blessings of that month. However, this view is decisively rejected because Allah ﷻ explicitly states in Surah Al-Baqarah: “The month of Ramadan is that in which the Quran was sent down” (2:185). Every night of Ramadan belongs within the revelation month, and this night must therefore be in Ramadan. The connection between the Quran’s revelation and Ramadan is ironclad.

Now let’s explore three essential meanings of the word قَدْر that frame our understanding of this surah.

First meaning: Decree and Calculation

One meaning of qadr in Arabic is calculation or measure—to set limits or determine the extent of something. Allah ﷻ uses this meaning when He says, “We calculated the stages for the moon” (قَدَّرَهُ مَنَازِلَ). Applied to Laylat al-Qadr, this is the night when Allah ﷻ sends down decisions—major decisions for the coming year. All the good decisions for the rest of the year are determined: who will earn what, who will experience what happiness, what child will be born, what marriage will take place, who will come to Islam, who will die a peaceful death. These matters are already known by Allah ﷻ, but the angels come to begin executing these decisions in this night.

Think of it like a government budget that gets approved and then executed. This is that night—when the heavenly decree made by Allah ﷻ is sent down and the decisions begin to be carried out by the angels.

This understanding is confirmed by what Allah ﷻ says in Surah Ad-Dukhan: “By the clear Book, indeed We sent it down during a blessed night—indeed, We were to warn. In it every precise matter is made distinct, as a command from Us. Indeed, We were to send a messenger, as mercy from your Lord” (44:2-6). Allah ﷻ describes this as the night when every wise decision is separated and distinguished—the night of great decision-making. These commands come from Allah ﷻ as mercy, not as calamity or destruction. They are loving commands filled with care.

And critically, Allah ﷻ ends that passage: “Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing” (44:6). He hears what’s going on inside your heart and the supplications you’re making, especially in this night.

Second meaning: Honor and Nobility

Another meaning of qadr is rank, nobility, or honor (مَنزِلَة or شَرَف). This is the night of great honor. Why? Consider what comes together: the most honorable of angels (Jibreel AS) came in this night, carrying the most honorable book of Allah ﷻ (the Quran), giving it to the most honorable messenger of Allah ﷻ (Muhammad ﷺ), in the most honored of nights. It’s a convergence of all these glories and honors coming together in Laylat al-Qadr. The first ayah—إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ—is describing one of the things that makes this night so noble and honorable.

Third meaning: Power

Qadr also means power or ability. This is a night in which we should have complete faith in the power of Allah ﷻ to change things—to change our fate, to transform our behavior, to empower us to accomplish what we couldn’t before. Allah ﷻ has endowed this night with unusual strength and power. After all, the greatest miracle ever given to humanity—the Quran—was given in this night. The most powerful Word of Allah ﷻ descended in this night. We should therefore have more belief in the miraculous answering of prayers this night than any other night of the year.

A rare fourth meaning: Congestion

There’s one more rare interpretation worth mentioning. The word qadr can also mean congestion or constraint—when something is tightly packed together. This meaning appears in the Quran when describing limited provision: “And whoever has his provision restricted” (وَمَن قُدِرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقُهُ). Applied here, some scholars suggest that so many angels descend on this night that the earth becomes congested with them. There are hadith indicating that more angels descend this night than there are grains of sand on the earth. This suggests that angels who normally remain in the heavens come down exclusively for this night. The world becomes so flooded with angels that there’s no room left for any evil. It’s completely filled with divine presence.

Among these interpretations, the most commonly emphasized—particularly in light of Surah Ad-Dukhan—is that this is the night of decree, when decisions are made for the coming year. And the greatest decision Allah ﷻ made for humanity—the coming of the final revelation—is also part of this night.

The scholars have called this the “anniversary of the Quran”—the night we celebrate the coming of Allah’s final message. This should be considered the Eid of the Quran.

Before moving forward, we should address an important question: Why didn’t Allah ﷻ tell us which specific night it is? There are narrations where the Prophet ﷺ remembered which night it was but was made to forget.

Imam ar-Razi and others offer profound wisdom here. The Prophet ﷺ once told someone else to wake a sleeping person for prayer instead of doing it himself. When asked why, he explained: “If I did it and he lazily refused or dismissed me, that would be an act of disbelief—defying the Messenger. But if you do it and he dismisses you, he’s just being lazy.”

Apply this wisdom to Laylat al-Qadr. If we knew with certainty that it was tonight, and we dismissed it or were “not in the mood,” that would be a major crime. After an entire surah has been revealed about how monumental this night is, to then casually skip it would be a grave violation. The greater something is, the greater its violation becomes.

It’s actually a mercy of Allah ﷻ that He didn’t specify the exact night. He didn’t open that door for us to commit such a crime. Instead, He made us humbly seek this night across multiple nights. May Allah ﷻ make us among those who seek this night with humility and find it.

Ayah 2

وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ

“And what can make you know what the Night of Power is?” This is Allah’s way of saying: “I’m about to tell you the main things you need to know about Laylat al-Qadr. I’m going to share with you the core concepts that you need to internalize so you truly value this night and never lose sight of its significance.”

This rhetorical question creates anticipation. It’s a teaching method—grabbing your attention before delivering crucial information. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: “You can’t fully grasp what this night really means, but let Me tell you what you need to understand.”

The structure of this surah is remarkable. After this question, every subsequent ayah provides a unique answer—a different dimension of qadr. If you look at the key words in each ayah, you’ll see the organizational beauty:

  • The first couple of ayahs: قَدْر (decree)
  • The third ayah: خَيْرٌ (better/goodness)
  • The fourth ayah: أَمْرٍ (command/matter)
  • The final ayah: سَلَـٰمٌ (peace)

Each of these connects to a different meaning of qadr: decree, value, command, and duration. The surah’s architecture is perfectly designed to answer the question “What is Laylat al-Qadr?” from multiple angles.

Ayah 3

لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ

“The Night of Power is better than a thousand months.” This is the first value Allah ﷻ assigns to this night—and remember, qadr also means value, so this is the night of value answering: What is its value?

Now, a thousand months calculated literally equals eighty-three years and four months. Some have taken this to mean that if you pray on this night, it’s as if you prayed for 83 years. But this doesn’t mean you can pray tonight and then take an 83-year vacation from prayer. That’s not how this works.

When Allah ﷻ mentions large numbers like “a thousand” in the Quran—like “a thousand years of what you count” (أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ)—He’s often indicating something beyond precise calculation. A thousand months essentially represents a full human lifetime. Eighty-some years is the average lifespan of a person.

So Allah’s way of saying this is: In your estimation, this night is better than an entire lifetime. If you find this night and take advantage of it, you’ve found an entire good life for yourself.

Think about the layers of meaning here. We were just discussing the decisions that will unfold from this year to the next. But Allah ﷻ goes much further: This night is so noble, so grand, so powerful that it can set the course for the rest of your life. It can actually change everything about your existence. Laylat al-Qadr isn’t just about the coming year—it can transform your entire future.

And notice: Allah ﷻ says خَيْرٌ مِّنْ—“better than.” He didn’t specify how much better. Is it a thousand times better than a thousand months? A million times better? He left it open, unqualified. He simply said it’s better than a thousand months. The thousand months is just what we can imagine, but the reality is far beyond even that.

One more note on language: qadr in Arabic also means “value,” so when Allah ﷻ asks “What is Laylat al-Qadr?” one meaning is “What is the night of value?” And the answer comes: Its value is more than a thousand months.

Ayah 4

تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ

“The angels and the Spirit descend in it by permission of their Lord for every matter.” After addressing the value of this night, Allah ﷻ now addresses another meaning of qadr: decree. This ayah deals with the commands of Allah ﷻ that come for every issue.

تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ—“The angels descend.” All the angels come down from their stations in the heavens. And specifically mentioned: وَٱلرُّوحُ—“and the Spirit.” This is Jibreel (AS), whom the Bible calls the Holy Spirit and the Quran calls Ar-Ruh. The angel who brought the Quran in the first place is now a special guest descending again.

Think about what we’re discussing here. The original star of revelation—the one who brought the Quran to begin with—celebrates the anniversary of the Night of the Quran by coming down again himself. And he’s described in the Quran as ذُو قُوَّةٍ—one of tremendous power, with a huge following. Not only Jibreel (AS), but his entire team that used to accompany him when bringing the Quran—all of them now descend together into the heavens and onto the earth.

This is the same Jibreel (AS) who brought revelation to Musa (AS), to Isa (AS), to all the prophets. And tonight he’s in our company—he and all his followers, along with all the other angels.

You can think of angels as having different departments. The first heaven has its angels, the second has its angels, each assigned to different tasks. Allah ﷻ describes angels who guard us, angels who record our deeds, angels who continuously glorify Him around the Throne. Angels are doing different things throughout creation.

But tonight? All the angels have been taken off their regular tasks and sent down. Even Jibreel (AS) descends from the highest station—from Sidrat al-Muntaha, from the presence of divine revelation itself—he comes down for this night.

The phrase بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم—“by permission of their Lord”—is significant. Angels have their own work to do, their own assigned tasks. Allah ﷻ has given them freedom from whatever they normally do to come here, to descend for this purpose. That’s why “by His permission” matters—this is a special dispensation, a unique night when the normal order is suspended for something greater.

مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ—“for every matter.” They come with every kind of command, every kind of issue. Whether it’s a health issue, a personal matter, a family concern, a matter of war or peace, a spiritual struggle, an emotional difficulty, a physical ailment, a financial problem—it doesn’t matter what kind of issue. Allah ﷻ has decreed that the angels come to address all kinds of issues and help people with all matters. They’re declaring the decree of Allah ﷻ, executing His commands for those who seek Him in this night.

The angels are all around us, hovering around us. They’ve flooded our space. They’re ready to deliver what Allah ﷻ has written for us.

One crucial grammatical point: Notice Allah ﷻ uses the present tense—تَنَزَّلُ (they descend)—not the past tense. This suggests this isn’t just describing that first time when the Quran was revealed. It keeps happening. This descent of the angels occurs every single year. It’s not a one-time historical event. It’s a gift Allah ﷻ gives to humanity annually, every Ramadan, every Laylat al-Qadr.

The scholars note that legions of angels descend, and among them the most noble. This is the gift of Allah ﷻ to the believers. Some commentators say the angels secure the commands of Allah ﷻ—they ensure His decree is executed properly.

Ayah 5

سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ

“Peace it is until the emergence of dawn.” As we arrive at this final ayah, Allah ﷻ gives us yet another meaning of qadr. Remember, qadr can mean “limit” or “measure”—as in the ayah “Allah has set a measure for everything” (قَدْ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدْرًا).

So beautifully organized: The first answer after the question “What is Laylat al-Qadr?” was about the value of this night (better than a thousand months). The second was about the decree of this night (angels descending with every command). And the third is about the limit or duration of this night—how long does it last?

And to me, this is the most fascinating aspect. Allah ﷻ says this night extends حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ—“until the emergence of dawn.” Notice He didn’t say until Fajr time begins, until the adhan is called, or until the first light appears on the horizon. He said until مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ—until the rising of dawn, when you can actually see the sunlight beginning to emerge, when the sky has turned and daylight is clearly coming.

This is remarkable. You know how our fast ends when it’s still relatively dark, at Maghrib? The sun has set but darkness remains. Similarly, Fajr begins when the first light appears but it’s still mostly dark. But Laylat al-Qadr? Allah ﷻ extended it. He made it continue until you start seeing actual light itself—until the dawn is emerging and rising.

He extended its value beyond the normal bounds of night. This is a unique gift of Laylat al-Qadr—Allah ﷻ enhanced its duration for us. Unlike any other night, He counts the early morning part—even as light begins to appear—as still part of this blessed night. That’s an extra gift, extra time for worship, extra time for mercy.

Now let’s examine the profound word سَلَـٰمٌ—peace. This is actually a very weighty word that dominates this surah. If you look at the key words throughout: there’s qadr (power/decree), khayr (goodness), amr (command), and finally salam (peace). All of them connect to the central theme.

Those familiar with even basic Arabic will notice something unusual here. The normal sentence structure would be هِىَ سَلَـٰمٌ—“It is peace.” But Allah ﷻ reversed the sequence: سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ—“Peace it is.” This reversal creates tremendous emphasis. It’s saying: This night—there is no peace like the peace of this night. There’s no night that has the kind of peace this night has. Peace like no other exists in it.

Before we go deeper into salam, I should mention something endearing that some scholars have observed—though others dismiss it as overly creative. Some people hold the view that Laylat al-Qadr is specifically the 27th night. They literally counted the letters: the first word إِنَّآ has letters, the second word أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ has letters, continuing until when you reach هِىَ, that’s the 27th letter in the surah. So they concluded: This is it—the 27th night.

Another numerical observation, also attributed to Ibn Abbas (RA): The words لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ consist of nine letters. These words appear three times in the surah. Nine times three equals 27—therefore the 27th night.

Some scholars say this is silly, that it has nothing to do with textual interpretation. But I mention it because it shows the depth of love and attention believers have given to studying this surah, searching for hints about this blessed night.

Now let’s truly understand سَلَـٰمٌ, because this is a tremendously significant word in Islamic theology.

Salam is one of the names of Allah ﷻ Himself: ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ—“The King, the Pure, the Peace” (59:23). Similarly, Jannah is called Dar as-Salam—the Abode of Peace, the Place of Peace. So peace is a name of Allah ﷻ and the name for Paradise itself.

But salam also appears in contexts of danger being removed. When Ibrahim (AS) was thrown into the fire, Allah ﷻ commanded: كُونِى بَرْدًا وَسَلَـٰمًا—“Be coolness and safety” (21:69). Become peace itself, eliminate the danger completely.

Now notice the remarkable subtlety in this ayah. Allah ﷻ didn’t say the night is سَالِمَة (peaceful)—He said سَلَـٰمٌ (peace itself). Grammatically, a night isn’t literally peace—a night is peaceful. There’s a difference. When we say “the garden is peaceful,” we’re using an adjective. But Allah ﷻ used an infinitive, an abstract noun: سَلَـٰمٌ—peace itself.

What does this mean? Not only is this night peaceful, but if you can truly take advantage of this night, you have found peace itself for the rest of your life. You’ve found ultimate, complete peace. It’s as if you haven’t truly experienced what peace means until you’ve found this night. That’s the magnitude of the statement being made.

There’s another dimension to consider. Salam can also function as a command. In English, a teacher might say “Silence!” instead of “Be silent!” A judge says “Order!” rather than “Be orderly!” Sometimes a noun itself issues a command.

Applied here, سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ becomes: Peace!—meaning you better be at peace. No fighting, no arguing, no conflicts. Allah ﷻ has sent the angels down, sent His decree down. This is the most valuable night of the year. You better be in complete submission before Allah ﷻ. You better be at peace with Him.

Salam is the opposite of war, of conflict. You better not be argumentative with anyone. You better not be harsh or snippy with others. Be good with Allah ﷻ and good to humanity. Be at peace and in submission this night. It’s like a divine suggestion—or really, a command—at the end: Until Fajr comes, drop every conflict, release every tension, let everything go. Just be at peace. سَلَـٰمٌ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ.

Others have drawn from this ayah guidance about recognizing Laylat al-Qadr: You’ll feel a special kind of peace in this night that you don’t feel otherwise. When you stand in prayer, you’ll experience a tranquility that makes you want to remain in prayer—a feeling you’ve never had before. It’s an unusual spiritual experience, the kind of calm Allah ﷻ gives a believer uniquely in this night. These are signs we look for in seeking Laylat al-Qadr.

Signs of Laylat al-Qadr from Hadith Literature

Let me share what we find in the hadith about seeking this night and recognizing it:

The Prophet ﷺ taught us that our lifespans, our provisions, everything about our coming year is decreed in this night. Angels descend with all that is good, with all blessings, with Allah’s complete mercy and forgiveness.

As mentioned, the angels that descend may be ones who only come for this specific night—they don’t descend at any other time. This is especially true of Jibreel (AS).

In one narration, the Prophet ﷺ said: “I was shown Laylat al-Qadr, then I was made to forget it. But it is in the last ten nights.” He described it as a calm night—not too hot, not too cold, with a brilliant moon and stars. Shaytan doesn’t emerge in it, and there’s peace until the morning.

People are unusually at peace this night. Even when you go to the masjid, there’s a different atmosphere—no one’s annoyed, no pushing and shoving, children aren’t causing chaos outside. Everything is calmer than usual. Something is different.

Some narrations mention the breeze is soft, and the rising sun has a gentle, reddish tone—it’s not intense when it appears.

Allah ﷻ may show a believer which night it is in their sleep, as happened with several companions (RA).

The Prophet ﷺ said: “The number of angels on this night exceeds the number of pebbles and grains of sand on the earth.” The earth is flooded with divine presence.

How to Worship in Laylat al-Qadr

Finally, the Prophet ﷺ taught us the greatest practice for this night: “Whoever stands in prayer during Laylat al-Qadr with faith and seeking reward, all their previous sins will be forgiven.”

Let’s understand the qualities of this prayer:

Imanan—out of faith. Pray driven by complete conviction that this prayer will be the best thing that’s happened to you. Have confidence and reliance on Allah ﷻ in these prayers like no other time.

Wa-htisaban—seeking reward, taking full account. Be aware of your sins, your flaws, what you’ve done, what you’re ashamed of before Allah ﷻ. Come before Allah ﷻ standing in prayer, ashamed and humiliated, yet completely convinced that He will forgive you, bless you, and let your sins pass. This is a confessional prayer—coming before Allah ﷻ humiliated, in tears, in full awareness of yourself.

If you can offer that kind of prayer—full of faith, full of self-awareness, full of hope in Allah’s mercy—then every embarrassing sin, every mistake you’ve made, is guaranteed to be forgiven. All of it.

This kind of prayer is harder to do in congregation. It’s much easier when you’re by yourself, alone with Allah ﷻ. If you’re someone who struggles to pray alone, then yes, come to the masjid and join the congregation. But this is the night where ideally you want it to be just you and Allah ﷻ, in tears, reciting Quran, making your supplications.

Become among those fortunate enough to have found this Laylat al-Qadr and found the forgiveness of Allah ﷻ in it.

We’re also fortunate that our nights aren’t very long anymore. There’s not that much time between Isha and Fajr. Get some rest. Pray. Rest again. Pray again. Rest. Pray. Alternate like this across these nights.

May Allah ﷻ help us all find Laylat al-Qadr. May He give us familiarity with His Book and grant us the tears, sincerity, and strength to truly worship Him in these blessed nights.

Ayah 1–5 · linked video: 01. Al-Qadr (Ayah 1-5)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

أَنزَلْنَاهُ (anzalnahu) [ن-ز-ل]

The root ن-ز-ل conveys the meaning of descent, coming down from a higher place to a lower one. The Form IV verb أَنزَلَ (anzala) means “to send down” or “to reveal,” emphasizing the divine origin of what descends. In this context, أَنزَلْنَاهُ (anzalnahu) means “We sent it down,” where the pronoun هُ (hu - “it”) refers to the Quran without explicitly naming it. This pronoun usage (called ḍamīr al-sha’n in Arabic rhetoric) indicates that the Quran was so prominent in the minds of the Makkans—both believers and disbelievers—that it needed no introduction. When someone said “it,” everyone knew what was meant. This grammatical choice demonstrates the Quran’s permeating presence in Makkan society.

The use of نَزَّلَ versus أَنزَلَ is significant in Quranic usage. While نَزَّلَ (Form II) often indicates gradual, repeated descent over time, أَنزَلَ (Form IV) can indicate a single, complete descent. This supports the interpretation from Ibn Abbas (RA) that the entire Quran descended from the seventh heaven to the first heaven in one night—this night of Al-Qadr—and then was gradually revealed to the Prophet ﷺ over 23 years. The first stage was إنزال (inzal - complete sending down), and the second stage was تنزيل (tanzil - gradual revelation).

لَيْلَةِ (laylati) [ل-ي-ل]

The root ل-ي-ل refers to night, the period of darkness between sunset and sunrise. The word لَيْلَة (laylah) is the feminine form meaning “a night” or “one night.” It is in the genitive case (majrur) here because it follows the preposition فِي (fi - “in”). The specificity of “a night” rather than just “night” emphasizes that this was one particular, distinct night among all nights—a night that would be set apart forever in human history.

الْقَدْرِ (al-qadri) [ق-د-ر]

The root ق-د-ر is extraordinarily rich in meaning, which gives this surah its depth. The root conveys multiple interconnected meanings:

  1. Decree/Determination: قَدَّرَ (qaddara) means to decree, determine, or ordain something. This connects to Allah’s divine decree and the decisions made for the coming year that are executed starting this night.

  2. Measurement/Calculation: قَدَرَ (qadara) means to measure, calculate, or set precise limits. Allah says قَدَّرَهُ منَازِلَ (qaddarahu manazila) - “We calculated stages for it [the moon]” (36:39). This meaning relates to how Allah measures out provisions, lifespans, and destinies.

  3. Power/Ability: قَدَرَ عَلَى (qadara ’ala) means to have power over something, to be capable. This connects to the miraculous power manifest in this night and Allah’s absolute power to change circumstances.

  4. Honor/Status/Value: قَدْر also means worth, value, or honor—as in the phrase مَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ (“They did not give Allah His true measure/worth”) (6:91). This meaning makes Laylat al-Qadr “The Night of Great Value” or “The Night of Honor.”

  5. Constraint/Congestion: قَدَّرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ means “He constrained his provision” (89:16). Some scholars suggest this relates to how the earth becomes “congested” with the countless angels descending this night—more angels than grains of sand on earth, according to hadith.

The word is in the genitive case (majrur) as the second part of an idafah construction (الإضافة), making it definite through its connection to لَيْلَة. All these meanings resonate simultaneously in the word, creating layers of significance for this night.

Ayah 2

أَدْرَاكَ (adraka) [د-ر-ك]

The root د-ر-ك means to reach, catch up with, or comprehend something. The Form IV verb أَدْرَكَ (adraka) means “to make one reach knowledge” or “to inform.” The phrase وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ (wa ma adraka) is a rhetorical device used throughout the Quran to emphasize the magnificence or gravity of what follows. It literally means “And what could make you know?” or “What could possibly give you a clue?” This expression appears before describing matters of immense importance—Day of Judgment, Hell, Paradise, or in this case, Laylat al-Qadr.

The structure creates suspense and prepares the listener’s mind to receive something extraordinary. It’s Allah’s way of saying: “What I’m about to tell you is so tremendous that you could never grasp it on your own—I must inform you directly.” The mā (مَا) here is interrogative, and the past tense verb suggests “what has made you know” or “what could ever make you know,” implying the impossibility of knowing without divine revelation.

Ayah 3

لَيْلَةُ (laylatu) [ل-ي-ل]

Here لَيْلَة appears in the nominative case (marfu’) as the subject (mubtada’) of a nominal sentence. This grammatical positioning gives it emphasis—“The Night of Al-Qadr” stands as the subject being described, with what follows being its predicate (khabar).

خَيْرٌ (khayrun) [خ-ي-ر]

The root خ-ي-ر means goodness, better, or best. The word خَيْر (khayr) in its basic form means “good” or “better,” and here it functions as the predicate. The tanwin (nunation) on خَيْرٌ indicates it is indefinite, which creates emphasis and magnitude—“better” without specifying exactly how much better, leaving it open-ended and unlimited.

The choice of خَيْر (comparative/superlative form) rather than حَسَن (good) or صَالِح (righteous) is deliberate. It invites comparison: this night is better THAN something else. The verse immediately answers: better than what? Better than a thousand months.

أَلْفِ (alfi) [ء-ل-ف]

The root ء-ل-ف relates to being familiar with, accustomed to, or the number thousand. أَلْف (alf) specifically means one thousand. It is in the genitive case (majrur) because it follows the preposition مِن (min - “than”). The word ألف in Arabic usage often indicates a large, complete number—a lifetime’s worth of something.

شَهْرٍ (shahrin) [ش-ه-ر]

The root ش-ه-ر means to make known, to publicize, or to become well-known. The word شَهْر (shahr) means “month,” derived from this root because the moon’s phases are visible and well-known markers of time. It is in the genitive case (majrur) as the second term of the idafah (أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ - “a thousand months”).

The calculation: 1,000 months = 83 years and 4 months, roughly a human lifetime. However, the meaning extends beyond mere calculation. Allah is saying this single night has more value than an entire lifetime of regular nights. Some scholars note that ألف also appears in phrases like أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ (“a thousand years of what you count”) (32:5), where it represents an incomprehensibly long time from human perspective. The use of مِن (“than”) without specifying exactly how much better leaves the superiority open-ended—it could be thousands of times better than a thousand months.

Ayah 4

تَنَزَّلُ (tanazzalu) [ن-ز-ل]

This is the Form V verb from the same root ن-ز-ل we encountered in Ayah 1. While Form IV (أَنزَلَ) means “to send down,” Form V (تَنَزَّلَ) carries the meaning of descending gradually, repeatedly, or in large numbers. The form suggests continuous, ongoing action. The present tense here (المضارع) indicates that this descent happens every year during Laylat al-Qadr, not just once when the Quran was first revealed. This answers a key question in tafsir: does this night’s blessing occur annually? Yes, the present tense confirms it.

The Form V also implies multiplicity—many angels descending together, wave after wave. This grammatical form beautifully captures the image of countless angels descending en masse to earth.

الْمَلَائِكَةُ (al-mala’ikatu) [م-ل-ك]

The root م-ل-ك relates to possessing, controlling, or having authority—the same root as مَلِك (king) and مُلْك (dominion). مَلَائِكَة (mala’ikah) is the plural of مَلَك (malak - angel). The word suggests beings who carry messages and execute commands—they possess authority delegated by Allah to carry out His will.

The angels are mentioned in the nominative case (marfu’) as the subject of تَنَزَّلُ. The definite article (ال) indicates ALL the angels—not just some, but the angelic realm in its totality participates in this night. This includes angels normally stationed in different heavens who don’t typically descend to earth.

الرُّوحُ (ar-ruhu) [ر-و-ح]

The root ر-و-ح relates to spirit, soul, breath, or relief. الرُّوح (ar-ruh) means “the Spirit,” and in Islamic understanding, this specifically refers to Jibreel (AS), the angel of revelation. He is called الرُّوح because he brings spiritual life through revelation, just as breath (روح) brings physical life. He is also called رُوح الْقُدُس (Ruh al-Qudus - “the Holy Spirit”) in the Quran.

The conjunction وَ (wa - “and”) separating الملائكة and الروح indicates that Jibreel (AS) is mentioned separately from “the angels” for special emphasis. He is singled out because he is the original bearer of the Quran to the Prophet ﷺ, so he returns every Laylat al-Qadr as a special guest, celebrating the anniversary of the Quran’s revelation. He is accompanied by his original team—the angels who used to descend with Quranic revelation.

بِإِذْنِ (bi-idhni) [ء-ذ-ن]

The root ء-ذ-ن means to permit, allow, or give permission. إِذْن (idhn) means permission or authorization. The preposition بِ (bi) here means “with” or “by means of,” so بِإِذْنِ means “by the permission of” or “with the authorization of.”

This phrase emphasizes that the angels—despite their exalted status—cannot act independently. They descend only when Allah permits them. They have their assigned tasks and stations, but on this night, Allah releases them from their usual duties to participate in Laylat al-Qadr. This shows both Allah’s sovereignty over His creation and the extraordinary nature of this night—it requires special divine permission for this cosmic event.

رَبِّهِم (rabbihim) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means to nurture, sustain, raise, or have lordship over something. رَبّ (rabb) means Lord, Master, Sustainer, Nurturer. It is in the genitive case (majrur) following the idafah structure, with the attached pronoun هِم (him - “their”) making it “their Lord.”

The choice of رَبّ rather than الله is significant. Allah often uses رَبّ when emphasizing His care, nurturing, and intimate relationship with His creation. The angels come by permission of their Rabb—the One who created them, sustains them, and directs them. This highlights the mercy and care embedded in this night’s decree.

كُلِّ (kulli) [ك-ل-ل]

The root ك-ل-ل indicates completeness, totality, or wholeness. كُلّ (kull) means “every” or “all.” It is in the genitive case (majrur) because it follows the preposition مِن (min - “from”). The word كُلّ emphasizes totality—EVERY kind of matter, EVERY type of decree, ALL issues.

أَمْرٍ (amrin) [ء-م-ر]

The root ء-م-ر means to command, order, or a matter/affair. أَمْر (amr) can mean either “a command” or “an affair/matter/issue.” Here it encompasses both meanings—the angels descend with divine commands (decrees of Allah) concerning every matter and affair for the coming year.

The word is indefinite (tanwin on أَمْرٍ) following كُلّ, creating the meaning “every kind of matter” or “all types of affairs.” This includes health, wealth, family, spiritual, worldly, individual, and collective matters. The phrase مِن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ (min kulli amrin - “concerning every matter”) indicates comprehensive divine decree—nothing is left out. This is the night when Allah’s decisions for the year are sent down to be executed by the angels.

Ayah 5

سَلَامٌ (salamun) [س-ل-م]

The root س-ل-م conveys meanings of safety, security, soundness, and peace—freedom from harm, defect, or disturbance. سَلَام (salam) is the verbal noun (masdar) meaning “peace” itself. The word appears in multiple Quranic contexts: as one of Allah’s names (السَّلَام - 59:23), as the name of Paradise (دَارُ السَّلَامِ - the Abode of Peace), and as a greeting of peace (السَّلَامُ عَلَيْكُمْ).

Grammatically, سَلَامٌ is in the nominative case (marfu’) and serves as the predicate (khabar) of an implied subject. The normal sentence structure would be هِيَ سَلَامٌ (hiya salamun - “it is peace”), but Allah fronted سَلَامٌ before هِيَ to create emphasis. This rhetorical device (taqdim al-khabar) emphasizes that this night is PEACE—not just peaceful, but peace itself.

Using the masdar (verbal noun) instead of an adjective is profound. Allah didn’t say the night is سَالِمَة (salimah - “peaceful” or “safe”). He said the night IS سَلَام—peace itself, the very essence of peace. This suggests that experiencing this night means experiencing what peace truly is. If you find this night, you find peace for your entire life. The night embodies peace in its absolute form.

The tanwin (indefinite nunation) on سَلَامٌ creates a sense of magnificence—“peace” beyond description or limitation. What kind of peace? Complete, perfect, absolute peace. Peace in every dimension—spiritual peace, emotional peace, peace from punishment, peace from past sins, peace from anxiety about provision, peace in decree.

The word سَلَام can also function as a command (اسم فعل أمر)—like when a judge says “Order!” or a teacher says “Silence!” Similarly, سَلَامٌ can mean “Be at peace!” This suggests a divine command: Let there be peace in this night. Be at peace with Allah. Be at peace with people. Drop all conflicts, grudges, and tensions. Submit peacefully to Allah in this blessed night.

حَتَّىٰ (hatta) [ح-ت-ي]

The particle حَتَّىٰ means “until” and indicates the endpoint or limit of something. It marks when the state described will continue up to a specific time. Here it tells us that the peace, blessings, and special status of this night continue until Fajr.

مَطْلَعِ (matla’i) [ط-ل-ع]

The root ط-ل-ع means to rise, appear, or emerge—especially used for the rising of the sun or moon. مَطْلَع (matla’) is a noun of place/time (اسم مكان/زمان) meaning “the rising place” or “the time of rising.” It is in the genitive case (majrur) after حَتَّىٰ.

The specific choice of مَطْلَعِ rather than طُلُوع (the verbal noun “rising”) is significant. It refers to the early moments when light begins to appear—not when the sun is fully up, but when dawn breaks and light emerges. This extends the blessed time of Laylat al-Qadr beyond what might be expected.

الْفَجْرِ (al-fajri) [ف-ج-ر]

The root ف-ج-ر means to split open, burst forth, or gush out. الفَجْر (al-fajr) means dawn—the time when darkness is “split open” by the emerging light. It is in the genitive case (majrur) as the second term of the idafah (مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ - “the rising of dawn”).

The significance of this ending time is profound. Normally, the Islamic night ends at Fajr when the second dawn (الفجر الصادق) appears and Fajr prayer begins—but the actual sun hasn’t risen yet; it’s still dark. However, Allah extends Laylat al-Qadr’s blessing until مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ—until you actually SEE the light emerging, which is later than the prayer time. This is an extraordinary mercy—Allah prolonged this night’s duration beyond the usual definition of “night” to give believers extra time to worship and seek His forgiveness. The night of power continues until you witness the dawn light itself, maximizing the opportunity for blessing.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

Pronoun Usage (الضمير المبهم)

The pronoun هُ (hu - “it”) in أَنزَلْنَاهُ creates what scholars call ḍamīr al-sha’n (the pronoun of great importance) or ḍamīr al-qissah (the pronoun of the story). This is a rhetorical device where something is referred to by a pronoun before being explicitly named, indicating that the referent is so well-known or important that it needs no introduction. When Allah says “We sent IT down,” every listener—believer and disbeliever—knew immediately that “it” referred to the Quran. This demonstrates the Quran’s overwhelming presence in Makkan consciousness. The revelation had become THE topic, THE phenomenon that dominated discussion and thought.

This is similar to how in English, during a major event, people might say “Did you see it?” and everyone knows what “it” refers to without specification—the game, the show, the incident that’s on everyone’s mind. The pronoun usage here shows that the Quran had achieved that level of prominence in society.

Royal Plural (نون العظمة)

Allah uses the first-person plural أَنزَلْنَا (anzalna - “We sent down”) throughout this surah. This is the royal “We” (nunu al-’azamah) used by kings and rulers to indicate majesty and grandeur. It emphasizes Allah’s greatness and sovereignty. The use of “We” rather than “I” or “He” in this context amplifies the magnitude of the act—this revelation is not a small matter but a cosmic event backed by divine majesty.

Active Voice Emphasis

Allah chose the active voice أَنزَلْنَاهُ (“We sent it down”) rather than a passive construction. This direct attribution emphasizes that Allah Himself is the source of revelation. In the previous surah (Al-’Alaq), the focus was on the Prophet’s ﷺ experience (“Read!”). Here, Allah shifts focus to His own direct action—“We sent it.” This removes any intermediary from the emphasis. Yes, Jibreel (AS) brought it, but the words are Mine, the decision is Mine, the power is Mine. This is MY revelation.

Emphatic Particle إِنَّ (Inna)

The ayah begins with إِنَّ (inna), one of the strongest emphatic particles in Arabic, roughly equivalent to “Indeed” or “Verily” or “Without doubt.” It transforms the nominal sentence that follows into an emphatic statement. The structure إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ (inna anzalnahu) creates double emphasis—both the particle inna and the plural pronoun “We” stress the certainty and majesty of this revelation.

Preposition فِي Indicating Containment

The preposition فِي (fi - “in”) indicates containment or occurrence within something. فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (fi laylati al-qadri - “in the Night of Al-Qadr”) emphasizes that the revelation occurred WITHIN this night, contained by it, making the night itself a vessel for this momentous event. This choice of preposition (rather than, say, يوم - “on the day”) creates a sense of the night embracing and holding this blessed revelation.

Ayah 2

Rhetorical Question (الاستفهام التعجيبي)

The phrase وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ (wa ma adraka ma laylatu al-qadri) employs a rhetorical question structure used for emphasis and amazement rather than actual inquiry. This construction appears throughout the Quran before describing matters of immense significance—Hell (وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْحُطَمَةُ - 104:5), the Day of Judgment (وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا يَوْمُ الدِّينِ - 82:17), and other tremendous realities.

The structure creates suspense and prepares the listener’s heart and mind. It’s as if Allah is saying: “You think you understand what Laylat al-Qadr is? You have no idea of its true magnitude. Let Me tell you.” The question is not seeking information but rather amplifying amazement and emphasizing that human comprehension cannot grasp this night’s greatness without divine explanation.

Double مَا (Ma) Structure

The repetition of مَا (ma) in وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا creates a powerful rhetorical effect. The first مَا is interrogative (“what?”), and the second مَا introduces the subject being asked about. This doubling intensifies the sense of wonder—“What could make you know what it is?” The second مَا effectively says “the reality of” or “the true nature of.” Together, they create a sense of profound mystery being unveiled.

Past Tense in Rhetorical Question

The verb أَدْرَاكَ (adraka) is in the past tense, which in this rhetorical construction adds to the emphasis. “What has made you know?” implies “What could possibly have made you know?” The past tense here suggests the impossibility of knowing without divine intervention—as if to say, “Through what means that have occurred could you possibly have grasped this reality?” The answer is: through no means. Only Allah can inform you of this.

Ayah 3

Fronting the Subject (تقديم المبتدأ)

The ayah begins with لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ (laylatu al-qadri) in the nominative case as the subject (mubtada’), followed by the predicate خَيْرٌ (khayrun). This is the natural Arabic word order for nominal sentences, but here it serves to answer the rhetorical question from the previous ayah. “What is Laylat al-Qadr? Laylat al-Qadr is better than a thousand months.” The structure provides the direct answer, satisfying the suspense created by the question.

Indefinite Predicate (التنكير للتعظيم)

The predicate خَيْرٌ (khayrun) is indefinite (has tanwin), which in Arabic rhetoric often indicates magnificence and emphasis (tanwin at-ta’zim). By leaving it indefinite, Allah suggests something vast, unlimited, and beyond precise description. It’s not just “the better” or “a specific better”—it’s an open-ended, unlimited “better-ness.” The indeterminacy creates a sense of boundlessness.

Comparative Structure with مِن (Min)

The preposition مِن (min) introduces the object of comparison (مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ - “than a thousand months”). In Arabic grammar, خَيْر (khayr) is from the comparative/superlative patterns (أفعل التفضيل), and it takes مِن to indicate what is being compared against. The structure “X is better than Y” is achieved through this construction.

Interestingly, Allah didn’t specify HOW MUCH better. He didn’t say “a thousand times better” or quantify the superiority. He simply said خَيْرٌ مِّنْ (“better than”), leaving the degree of superiority open and unlimited. This rhetorical choice suggests that the night’s superiority over a thousand months cannot be quantified—it vastly exceeds that threshold.

Symbolism of Numbers (دلالة الأعداد)

The number أَلْف (alf - “one thousand”) represents completion and an extensive period in Arabic usage. One thousand months equals approximately 83 years and 4 months—essentially a full human lifetime. The rhetorical force of this comparison is: “This single night is worth more than an entire human life of regular nights.”

The choice of “months” (شَهْر) rather than “years” is also significant. Months are marked by the visible phases of the moon—they’re the natural way Arabs measured time. Using “months” makes the number larger (1,000 rather than 83), which emphasizes magnitude. Additionally, nights are part of months, so comparing a night to months creates a natural parallel.

Ayah 4

Present Tense for Recurring Action (المضارع للتجدد)

The verb تَنَزَّلُ (tanazzalu) is in the present tense (المضارع), which in Arabic indicates ongoing, recurring, or continuous action. This grammatical choice confirms that the descent of angels on Laylat al-Qadr is not a one-time historical event but occurs annually. The present tense answers a major interpretive question: Does this night’s blessing happen every year? Yes, the angels descend every year during Laylat al-Qadr.

Form V Pattern (التضعيف)

The Form V pattern of تَنَزَّلُ (tanazzalu) with the doubled middle letter (نزّل) conveys intensive, repeated, or gradual action. While Form I نَزَلَ (nazala) means “to descend” simply, and Form IV أَنزَلَ (anzala) means “to send down,” Form V تَنَزَّلَ (tanazzala) suggests descending in waves, gradually, in large numbers, or with intensity. This form beautifully captures the image of countless angels descending en masse, group after group, wave after wave throughout the night.

Conjunction وَ (Wa) Separating Angels and Ruh

The structure الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ (al-mala’ikatu wa ar-ruhu - “the angels and the Spirit”) uses the conjunction وَ (wa - “and”) to separate them. This grammatical choice indicates that الرُّوح (ar-ruh - Jibreel AS) is being distinguished from “the angels” for special mention, even though he is himself an angel. This rhetorical device (’atf khass ’ala ’amm - specifying after generalizing) highlights Jibreel’s unique status. It’s similar to saying “The people and the president arrived”—yes, the president is a person, but he’s singled out for honor.

This separation emphasizes Jibreel’s special role as the bearer of revelation, giving him distinguished mention in the verse. He is the star guest, the original messenger of the Quran, returning to celebrate the anniversary of the revelation he first brought.

Preposition بِ Indicating Accompaniment

The preposition بِ (bi) in بِإِذْنِ (bi-idhni) indicates accompaniment or instrumentality—“with” or “by means of.” The angels descend “with the permission of their Lord.” This grammatical structure emphasizes that their descent is not autonomous but authorized, sanctioned, and orchestrated by Allah. Despite their exalted status, angels are utterly dependent on divine permission for their actions.

Possessive Pronoun Indicating Relationship

The attached pronoun هِم (him - “their”) in رَبِّهِم (rabbihim - “their Lord”) creates a relationship of belonging and care. The angels are connected to Allah as their رَبّ (Rabb - Lord/Master/Sustainer). This genitive construction emphasizes the nurturing relationship—these are not rogue angels or independent agents, but beloved servants acting under their Master’s loving direction.

مِن for Partitioning (التبعيض)

The preposition مِن (min) in مِن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ (min kulli amrin) indicates partitioning or specification (بيانية). The phrase means “concerning every matter” or “regarding all affairs.” The مِن here clarifies what the angels descend with—they carry divine decrees concerning every conceivable matter for the year ahead. It’s a partitive meaning: “from among all matters” or “in every matter,” encompassing health, provision, life, death, marriage, children, spiritual states, worldly affairs—nothing is excluded.

Indefinite After كُلّ for Generalization

The word أَمْرٍ (amrin - “matter”) comes indefinite (with tanwin) after كُلِّ (kulli - “every”), which is a common Arabic structure for emphasis and generalization (التعميم). When كُلّ is followed by an indefinite noun, it means “every kind of” or “all types of”—emphasizing comprehensive inclusion. No category of decree is left out; the angels carry decisions about every affair imaginable.

Ayah 5

Fronting the Predicate (تقديم الخبر)

The word سَلَامٌ (salamun - “peace”) is fronted before the implied pronoun هِيَ (hiya - “it”), creating the structure سَلَامٌ هِيَ (salamun hiya) rather than the typical order هِيَ سَلَامٌ (hiya salamun). This rhetorical device (taqdim al-khabar ‘ala al-mubtada’) creates emphasis and restriction—it emphasizes PEACE as the defining characteristic of this night. The fronting suggests: “This night is nothing but peace” or “Peace—that’s what this night is.”

This structure is similar to saying in English “Mine is the house” instead of “The house is mine”—the fronting emphasizes ownership/characteristic strongly. Here, the fronting of سَلَامٌ emphasizes that peace is the essence, the reality, the core nature of this blessed night.

Using Masdar (المصدر) Instead of Adjective

Allah used the verbal noun (masdar) سَلَامٌ (salamun - “peace” itself) rather than an adjective like سَالِمَة (salimah - “peaceful” or “safe”). This is a profound rhetorical choice. The night is not merely peaceful—it IS peace itself. The night embodies peace in its essence. This is like saying “She is beauty” rather than “She is beautiful”—it elevates the description from an attribute to an essence.

Using the masdar creates a more absolute, more intense meaning. The night doesn’t just have peace; it is the very manifestation and essence of peace. Experiencing this night means experiencing what peace truly is in its purest form.

Indefinite for Magnificence (التنكير للتعظيم)

The tanwin (indefinite nunation) on سَلَامٌ creates a sense of grandeur and magnificence. What kind of peace? Complete peace. Perfect peace. Unlimited peace. Divine peace. The indefiniteness leaves it open and expansive—peace beyond description, beyond limitation, beyond what can be fully articulated. Not “the peace” (definite) but “peace” (indefinite and magnificent).

Dual Function: Nominal Sentence and Command

Grammatically, سَلَامٌ functions as a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية) meaning “It is peace.” However, it can also be understood as what’s called ism fi’l amr (اسم فعل أمر)—a noun functioning as a command. In this reading, سَلَامٌ means “Let there be peace!” or “Peace!” (as a command), similar to how a judge says “Order!” or a teacher says “Silence!”

In this interpretation, Allah is commanding peace in this night—not just describing it. Be at peace. Drop conflicts. Cease hostilities. Let go of grudges. Submit peacefully to Allah. This dual reading enriches the ayah: the night is peace (description), and believers should be at peace in it (prescription).

حَتَّىٰ (Hatta) Indicating Limit

The particle حَتَّىٰ (hatta - “until”) is called a harf ghayah (حرف غاية) or “particle of limit”—it marks the endpoint or boundary of something. It indicates that the peace, blessing, and special status of this night continue up to a specific point: the rising of Fajr.

Extended Time Frame (توسعة الوقت)

The phrase حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ (hatta matla’i al-fajr - “until the rising of dawn”) extends the blessed time beyond what might be expected. Normally, night ends at Fajr when the second dawn appears and it’s time for Fajr prayer—but the sun hasn’t actually risen yet; it’s still dark. However, مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ refers to the actual emergence of light, when you SEE the dawn breaking and light appearing.

This is an extraordinary mercy—Allah prolonged this night’s blessing beyond the strict definition of “night.” The night of power continues until you witness the light itself emerging from the horizon. This gives believers extra time to pray, make du’a, seek forgiveness, and worship. It’s as if Allah is squeezing every possible moment of blessing into this night, extending it mercifully so no opportunity is lost.

Genitive Construction (الإضافة)

The phrase مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ (matla’i al-fajr) is an idafah (genitive construction) meaning “the rising place/time of dawn.” This construction creates specificity and emphasis. It’s not just dawn (الفجر) but the precise moment of dawn’s emergence (مطلع الفجر)—the breaking, the splitting open of darkness, the first rays appearing. This precision emphasizes the extended mercy of this night’s duration.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 1

The First Revelation

Historically, Laylat al-Qadr marks the night when the Quran began to be revealed to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in 610 CE, when he was 40 years old. This occurred in the Cave of Hira on Jabal al-Nur (Mountain of Light) near Makkah during Ramadan. The Prophet ﷺ had been engaging in spiritual retreats (تحنث - tahannuth) in this cave, seeking truth away from the idolatry and moral corruption of Makkan society.

On this night, Jibreel (AS) appeared to him with the first five verses of Surah Al-’Alaq: “Read in the name of your Lord who created…” (96:1-5). This marked the beginning of prophethood and the start of a 23-year revelation process that would transform Arabia and human history.

Ibn Abbas’s Report on the Two-Stage Revelation

According to Ibn Abbas (RA), a close companion of the Prophet ﷺ and one of the foremost scholars of Quranic interpretation, the revelation occurred in two stages: the entire Quran descended from the Seventh Heaven (al-sama’ al-sabi’ah) to the First Heaven (al-sama’ al-dunya) in one night—Laylat al-Qadr. Then, from the First Heaven, it was revealed gradually to the Prophet ﷺ over 23 years through Jibreel (AS).

Scholars incline toward accepting this as marfu’ (attributed to the Prophet ﷺ) even though Ibn Abbas (RA) doesn’t explicitly quote the Prophet ﷺ, because matters of the unseen (الغيب) can only be known through revelation. Ibn Abbas (RA) wouldn’t speak confidently about the movements of the Quran through the heavens based on personal opinion—he must have learned this from the Prophet ﷺ himself.

Which Night is Laylat al-Qadr?

Historically, there has been significant scholarly discussion about which specific night is Laylat al-Qadr. Some scholars counted as many as 40 different opinions (even though there are only 30 days maximum in Ramadan, showing the diversity of views). Imam ar-Razi counted nine major opinions. Various companions reported different nights: the 13th, 19th, 21st, 23rd, 27th, or 29th.

A famous hadith reports that the Prophet ﷺ was shown which night it is but was then made to forget it. There is wisdom in this forgetting—it ensures that believers seek it throughout the last ten nights rather than focusing on just one and potentially missing it. This keeps worship consistent and intense throughout the period.

The strongest position, supported by numerous hadith, is that Laylat al-Qadr occurs in the last ten nights of Ramadan, with particular emphasis on the odd nights (21st, 23rd, 25th, 27th, 29th). The Prophet ﷺ said: “Seek it in the last ten nights of Ramadan” and “Seek it among the odd nights.” Many scholars lean toward the 27th night, but the uncertainty is divinely ordained mercy, as explained in the lecture.

Historical Context of Makkan Society

When this surah was revealed, the Quran had become a phenomenon in Makkah. Despite opposition from the Quraysh leadership, the Quran dominated discussions. Even enemies of Islam couldn’t stop thinking about it, talking about it, and reacting to it. The phrase إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ (“Indeed We sent IT down”) with the pronoun “it” (without explicitly saying “Quran”) demonstrates how ubiquitous the Quran had become—everyone knew what “it” referred to.

The Quraysh tried to suppress the Quran by forbidding people from listening to it, mocking it, and creating noise when it was recited. Yet they themselves would secretly listen to it at night. The Quran had penetrated Makkan consciousness so thoroughly that the pronoun alone sufficed to identify it.

Biblical Context: Divine Revelation at Night

The concept of divine revelation occurring at night has precedent in biblical tradition, though not identical. God spoke to various prophets in visions and dreams at night:

  • Allah spoke to Musa (Moses AS) at Mount Sinai, giving him the Torah. While the Quran doesn’t specify this occurred at night, Islamic tradition recognizes the Torah as divinely revealed scripture.
  • The Gospel (Injeel) was revealed to ’Isa (Jesus AS), though the exact circumstances aren’t detailed in the Quran.
  • Many prophets received visions at night: Ibrahim (Abraham AS) received the command to sacrifice his son in a dream/vision, Yusuf (Joseph AS) had prophetic dreams, and dreams played significant roles in prophetic experiences.

However, the Quran distinguishes its revelation clearly from previous scriptures. While earlier scriptures underwent alteration over time, the Quran has been preserved precisely as revealed. The emphasis on this night being “better than a thousand months” establishes the Quran’s revelation as the pinnacle of divine communication with humanity.

Ayah 2

No specific historical or biblical content related to this ayah in the lecture beyond what is covered in the discussion of Ayah 1 about the identification of the night.

Ayah 3

Calculating a Lifetime

The mention of “a thousand months” (approximately 83 years and 4 months) represents a complete human lifetime. In the 7th century Arabian context, this represented more than most people’s actual lifespan. Average life expectancy was shorter due to harsh desert conditions, diseases, warfare, and limited medical knowledge. By mentioning a thousand months, Allah is speaking of a full, complete human life from birth to old age—something many wouldn’t even experience in full.

This makes the comparison even more striking: one night worth more than an entire extended human lifetime. This would have resonated powerfully with the original audience, emphasizing the extraordinary value of this single night.

Ayah 4

Angels in Islamic Understanding

Angels (ملائكة) are beings created from light (نور), as mentioned in hadith. They are genderless, sinless, and created to worship and obey Allah perfectly. They never disobey a command they receive. Different angels have different roles: Jibreel (AS) is the angel of revelation, Mikael (AS) is associated with provision and natural phenomena, Israfeel (AS) will blow the trumpet for the Day of Judgment, and Izraeel (Malak al-Mawt) is the angel of death.

Angels don’t normally descend to earth in such vast numbers. They have assigned stations in the seven heavens and specific tasks. On Laylat al-Qadr, they are given special permission (إذن) to leave their stations and descend en masse to the earth. This is comparable to a cosmic holiday or celebration in the angelic realm—they come to honor the anniversary of the Quran’s revelation and to bring divine decrees for the coming year.

Jibreel (AS): The Spirit

Jibreel (AS), called الرُّوح (ar-Ruh - the Spirit) or رُوح الْقُدُس (Ruh al-Qudus - the Holy Spirit), is the most honored of angels. He is the messenger between Allah and His prophets. In Islamic tradition, he brought revelation to all prophets:

  • He brought the Torah to Musa (AS)
  • He brought the Psalms to Dawud (AS)
  • He brought the Gospel to ’Isa (AS)
  • He brought the Quran to Muhammad ﷺ

He appeared to the Prophet ﷺ in his true angelic form only twice—once at the beginning of revelation and once during the Night Journey (Isra and Mi’raj). Usually, he appeared in human form, often resembling the Companion Dihyah al-Kalbi (RA), who was known for his handsome appearance.

On Laylat al-Qadr, Jibreel (AS) returns to earth annually with his entourage of angels, celebrating the anniversary of the night he first brought divine revelation to humanity. His special mention emphasizes his unique status and the connection between this night and the Quran’s revelation.

Biblical Parallel: The Holy Spirit

In Christian scripture, the Holy Spirit is considered part of the Trinity (a concept Islam rejects). However, the Quran acknowledges that Allah strengthened ’Isa (AS) with Ruh al-Qudus (the Holy Spirit): “We gave ’Isa, son of Maryam, clear signs and strengthened him with the Holy Spirit” (2:87, 2:253). Islamic scholars interpret this as referring to Jibreel (AS), who supported and protected ’Isa (AS) throughout his mission.

This creates a connection across prophetic traditions: the same angel who supported ’Isa (AS) is the one who brought revelation to Muhammad ﷺ, emphasizing continuity in divine guidance.

The Decree (القدر)

The concept of divine decree on this night connects to a broader Islamic theological principle: قَضَاء وَ قَدَر (qada and qadar - divine will and decree). Allah’s knowledge encompasses all things, and He decrees what will occur. On Laylat al-Qadr, the angels receive instructions about what is to unfold in the coming year—who will be born, who will die, who will receive provision, who will face trials, who will find guidance, etc.

This is referenced in Surah Ad-Dukhan (44:3-5): “Indeed, We sent it down during a blessed night… On that night is made distinct every precise matter—a matter from Us. Indeed, We were to send messengers.” This “making distinct” (يُفْرَقُ - yufraq) of matters refers to the angels being given clear instructions about executing divine decrees.

However, Islamic theology maintains that this doesn’t contradict human free will. The decrees are based on Allah’s perfect knowledge of what people will freely choose. This night represents the execution phase of what Allah has eternally known and willed.

Ayah 5

Signs of Laylat al-Qadr

Various hadith describe signs by which Laylat al-Qadr might be recognized:

  1. A Calm Night: The Prophet ﷺ described it as “لَيْلَةٌ سَمِحَةٌ” (laylah samihah) - a calm, pleasant night, neither extremely hot nor cold. There’s an unusual sense of tranquility in the atmosphere.

  2. Moon and Stars: In some narrations, the moon is described as being like a flat dish (قَعْب), clear and bright. The stars shine brilliantly.

  3. No Shooting Stars: Satan and evil jinn don’t appear this night—there’s complete peace and safety from evil forces.

  4. Morning Sun: The sun rises on the next morning without intense rays, appearing slightly reddish and gentle rather than harsh and blazing (كَأَنَّهَا طَسْتٌ - “as if it is a shallow basin”).

  5. Personal Spiritual Experience: Believers may experience unusual peace in prayer, a strong desire to continue worshipping, an unexpected flow of tears, or dreams indicating the night.

Important note: Not all these signs are universal. Geographic variations (different climates, seasons opposite in different hemispheres, cloud cover, etc.) mean these signs cannot be absolutely applied everywhere. They are indicators rather than absolute proof.

The Prophet’s Practice

Historically, the Prophet ﷺ would engage in i’tikaf (spiritual retreat) during the last ten days of Ramadan, secluding himself in the masjid to focus entirely on worship. This practice was specifically to seek Laylat al-Qadr. After his passing, his wives (may Allah be pleased with them) continued this practice, and it became a sunnah for the community.

’A’ishah (RA) asked the Prophet ﷺ: “O Messenger of Allah, what should I say if I know which night is Laylat al-Qadr?” He ﷺ taught her a specific du’a: “اللَّهُمَّ إِنَّكَ عَفُوٌّ تُحِبُّ الْعَفْوَ فَاعْفُ عَنِّي” (Allahumma innaka ’afuwwun tuhibbu al-’afwa fa’fu ’anni - “O Allah, You are Pardoning and You love to pardon, so pardon me”). This became the signature prayer for Laylat al-Qadr.

Historical Understanding of “A Thousand Months”

Some scholars from the early generations provided historical context for the “thousand months” comparison. They noted that this represented more than 83 years—longer than most prophets’ missions among their people. This single night grants more spiritual benefit than decades of sustained prophetic work. It’s a divine gift of compression: concentrated blessing and opportunity condensed into hours.

Another historical perspective: before Islam, Arabs would engage in i’tikaf during Ramadan as inherited from the Abrahamic tradition, but they didn’t have the understanding of Laylat al-Qadr’s specific merit. Islam revived and clarified this practice, making explicit what was implicit in earlier traditions.

Coherence

Ayah 1

Connection to Previous Surah (Surah Al-’Alaq)

The previous surah, Al-’Alaq (96), begins with the first revelation: “Read in the name of your Lord who created…” It describes the Prophet’s ﷺ first experience receiving revelation—Jibreel (AS) commanding him to read, the personal struggle, and the beginning of his mission. That surah focuses on the human experience of receiving revelation.

Surah Al-Qadr now shifts perspective to the divine side of revelation. Instead of “Read!” (command to the Prophet ﷺ), it says “We sent it down” (Allah’s direct action). While Al-’Alaq focuses on what the Prophet ﷺ experienced, Al-Qadr reveals what Allah did. This creates a beautiful complementary pair: the human reception of revelation (Al-’Alaq) and the divine sending of revelation (Al-Qadr).

The connection is also temporal: Al-’Alaq describes WHAT happened (the first words revealed), while Al-Qadr describes WHEN it happened (in Laylat al-Qadr) and WHY it matters (the night’s unprecedented value).

Connection to Following Surah (Surah Al-Bayyinah)

The next surah, Al-Bayyinah (98), begins: “Those who disbelieved among the People of the Scripture and the polytheists were not to be parted until there came to them clear evidence—a Messenger from Allah, reciting purified scriptures.” This “clear evidence” (bayyinah) is precisely what came down on Laylat al-Qadr—the Quran itself.

So the sequence flows: Al-Qadr explains when and how the clear evidence came down, and Al-Bayyinah describes the response to that evidence and its impact on different groups. One surah announces the event; the next describes its consequences.

Opening Emphasis (إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ)

The surah opens with the emphatic particle إِنَّ (inna) and the royal plural “We,” creating immediate emphasis and majesty. This opening style is characteristic of Makki surahs that announce major revelations about divine actions. It parallels openings like “Indeed, We sent Nuh to his people” (71:1) or “Indeed, We have given you abundance” (108:1)—declarations of Allah’s decisive interventions.

The Pronoun “It” Creates Cohesion

The pronoun هُ (hu - “it”) in the first ayah creates cohesion across multiple surahs. Readers coming from Al-’Alaq already know “it” refers to the Quran. This pronoun links back to the previous revelation experience while moving forward to explain its timing and significance. It’s a bridge connecting what was revealed (Al-’Alaq’s first verses) with when it was revealed (Laylat al-Qadr).

Ayah 2

Rhetorical Question Structure in the Quran

The phrase وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ (wa ma adraka) appears multiple times throughout the Quran, always introducing matters of tremendous importance:

  • “What will make you know what the striking calamity is?” (101:3) - about the Day of Judgment
  • “What will make you know what the steep path is?” (90:12) - about acts of charity and righteousness
  • “What will make you know what the Night of Decree is?” (97:2) - this ayah
  • “What will make you know what the crushing fire is?” (104:5) - about Hell

This creates a thematic pattern: whenever Allah uses this phrase, He’s about to reveal something of ultimate significance that human intellect cannot grasp independently. The repetition across the Quran trains the reader to recognize: “When I hear this phrase, something tremendously important follows.”

Creating Suspense and Engagement

This ayah creates suspense between the first ayah’s declaration and the third ayah’s explanation. Instead of immediately saying “We sent it down in Laylat al-Qadr, which is better than a thousand months,” Allah inserts this rhetorical question. This pause forces the listener to anticipate, to wonder, to engage mentally and emotionally with what comes next.

This technique is pedagogically powerful: it primes the mind to receive information, making the subsequent answer more impactful and memorable. Teachers use this technique—asking a question before giving an answer increases retention and emotional impact.

Parallel to Surah Ad-Dukhan

This rhetorical questioning parallels Surah Ad-Dukhan’s opening: “Ha, Meem. By the clear Book, indeed We sent it down during a blessed night” (44:1-3). Both surahs deal with the Quran’s revelation in a special night, both emphasize the night’s blessing, and both create emphasis through rhetorical structure. Ad-Dukhan provides additional details about what happens in this night: “On that night is made distinct every precise matter” (44:4), which complements Al-Qadr’s focus.

Together, these two surahs provide the Quran’s complete teaching on Laylat al-Qadr: Al-Qadr focuses on its spiritual value for worship, while Ad-Dukhan focuses on the divine decree that occurs in it.

Ayah 3

Direct Answer to the Rhetorical Question

This ayah provides the direct answer to the previous ayah’s question. The structure is: Question: “What is Laylat al-Qadr?” Answer: “Laylat al-Qadr is better than a thousand months.” This question-answer format creates emphasis through anticipation and fulfillment.

The repetition of “Laylat al-Qadr” three times in the first three ayahs (verses 1, 2, and 3) drums the name into consciousness, making it unforgettable. The name appears once in declaration, once in question, and once in answer—a complete rhetorical cycle.

Thematic Connection: Value and Honor

This ayah addresses one dimension of the word qadr: value. Since qadr means both “decree” and “value,” the surah addresses both meanings. This ayah focuses on value: “How valuable is this night? More valuable than a thousand months.” The next ayah will focus on decree: “What is decreed? Angels descend with every command.”

This thematic organization—answering different dimensions of the surah’s central concept—creates internal cohesion. Each ayah builds on the previous one, exploring another facet of the same jewel.

Connection to the Quran’s Self-Description

Throughout the Quran, Allah describes His revelation with superlatives: “the best of speech” (39:23), “guidance for those conscious of Allah” (2:2), “a healing and mercy for believers” (17:82). The night of its revelation being “better than a thousand months” fits this pattern—both the Book and the night of its revelation are described in supreme terms.

This creates coherence in the Quran’s self-presentation: the greatest Book was revealed on the greatest night by the greatest angel to the greatest messenger. Every element is described in superlative terms, creating a consistent picture of divine grandeur.

Ayah 4

Shift from Value to Decree

After establishing the night’s value (better than a thousand months), this ayah shifts to explaining WHY it has such value: because angels descend with divine commands. The progression is logical: statement of value followed by explanation of value’s source.

This is how the Quran often structures its arguments: claim followed by evidence, or description followed by explanation. The night isn’t arbitrarily valuable; it’s valuable BECAUSE of what occurs in it—the descent of angels with commands from Allah.

Angels and Commands: Connection to Surah Ad-Dukhan

This ayah’s mention of angels descending “with every command” connects directly to Surah Ad-Dukhan: “On that night is made distinct every precise matter—a command from Us” (44:4-5). Ad-Dukhan specifies that commands are made distinct (يُفْرَقُ - yufraq), meaning separated, clarified, and assigned to angels for execution. Al-Qadr confirms angels descend with these commands.

Reading both surahs together provides a complete picture: In Ad-Dukhan, we learn that decisions are made distinct; in Al-Qadr, we learn that angels descend to execute them. The two surahs complement each other, each providing pieces of the same puzzle.

The Special Mention of Ar-Ruh (Jibreel)

Separating الرُّوح (ar-Ruh - Jibreel) from الْمَلَائِكَة (al-mala’ikah - the angels) creates emphasis and honor for Jibreel (AS). This rhetorical separation (عطف الخاص على العام - ’atf al-khass ’ala al-’amm) is used throughout the Quran when Allah wants to honor something after mentioning a general category.

For example: “Whoever is an enemy to Allah and His angels and His messengers and Jibreel and Mikael…” (2:98)—angels are mentioned generally, then Jibreel and Mikael specifically, honoring them. Similarly here: angels are mentioned, then Jibreel specially, honoring his role as the original bearer of revelation.

This creates thematic coherence with the surah’s opening: the surah began by referencing the Quran’s revelation (“We sent IT down”), and now it honors the angel who brought that revelation by giving him special mention.

Permission (بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم) Creates Cohesion

The phrase “by permission of their Lord” establishes that everything occurring this night is under divine control and authorization. This connects to the surah’s opening emphasis on divine action (“WE sent it down”). The surah maintains focus on Allah’s sovereignty: He sent the revelation, He grants permission for angels to descend, He issues the commands they carry. Every verb and action points back to divine will and authority.

This creates thematic unity: the surah is ultimately about Allah’s power and mercy, manifested through this special night.

Ayah 5

Concluding with Peace After Command

The surah’s progression is: declaration → question → value → decree → peace. After explaining what descends (commands from Allah), the surah concludes with the nature of those commands: they are سَلَام (salam - peace). This ending provides emotional resolution—after building tension through emphasis, questions, and cosmic events, the surah concludes with tranquility.

This mirrors the surah’s content: the night itself begins with intensity (angels descending, commands issued) but concludes with peace (سَلَامٌ هِيَ - “It is peace”). The surah’s structure mirrors the night’s structure.

Until Dawn: Establishing the Night’s Limit

Ending with “until the rising of dawn” establishes boundaries for the night, answering the implicit question: “How long does this blessing last?” This completes the surah’s comprehensive description: we know WHAT the night is (Laylat al-Qadr), WHEN it is (in Ramadan), HOW VALUABLE it is (better than a thousand months), WHAT HAPPENS in it (angels descend with commands), and WHEN IT ENDS (at dawn).

This thoroughness creates satisfaction—the listener’s questions are comprehensively answered. The surah leaves no loose ends.

Peace as the Culmination

The final word سَلَام (salam - peace) creates powerful thematic closure. The surah began with Allah’s action (أَنزَلْنَا - “We sent down”), continued through divine power and angelic activity, and concludes with the purpose of all that activity: peace for humanity. This progression—from divine action to human benefit—reflects the Quran’s consistent pattern of showing how Allah’s power serves His mercy.

The surah’s structure thus embodies its message: Allah’s night of decree is ultimately a night of peace, and all His commands are for human wellbeing.

Connection to the Prophet’s Practice

The surah’s ending “until the rising of dawn” connects to the Sunnah of extending worship through the entire night until Fajr. The Prophet ﷺ would pray throughout these nights, and the surah’s ending validates this practice—the blessing continues until dawn itself, so one should continue worship until then.

This creates coherence between revelation and practice, between the Quran’s words and the Prophet’s ﷺ actions, showing how the Sunnah implements and embodies Quranic teachings.

Final Cohesion with Surah Al-’Alaq

The surah ends with peace (سَلَام), while Al-’Alaq (the preceding surah) ends with prostration: “No! Do not obey him. But prostrate and draw near [to Allah]” (96:19). The sequence creates a beautiful flow: prostrate to Allah (Al-’Alaq’s ending) in the blessed night (Al-Qadr’s content) and find peace (Al-Qadr’s ending). The two surahs together prescribe both action (prostration) and its result (peace).

Overall Surah Structure and Coherence

The surah follows a clear five-part structure:

  1. Declaration (Ayah 1): We sent it down in Laylat al-Qadr
  2. Question (Ayah 2): What is Laylat al-Qadr?
  3. Value (Ayah 3): Better than a thousand months
  4. Decree (Ayah 4): Angels descend with commands
  5. Peace (Ayah 5): It is peace until dawn

This structure moves from announcement → wonder → explanation → elaboration → conclusion. Each ayah builds on the previous one, and each explores a different dimension of the central concept (qadr: value, decree, limit). This creates a tightly unified composition where every word contributes to the whole, and the whole is greater than its parts.
